stem: Find the slope of the line through \( ([[x1]],[[y1]]) \) and \( ([[x2]],[[y2]]) \).
Find the slope of the line \( [[A]]x+[[B]]y=[[C]] \).
solution: Solving for \( y \): \( y=-\frac{A}{B}x+\frac{C}{B} \), so the slope is \( -\frac{A}{B}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required minus sign.
This inverts the ratio, using \( B/A\) instead of \( A/B\).
This drops the minus sign and inverts the ratio.
This mistakenly multiplies by the constant term \( C\).
This is off by one from the correct slope.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct slope.

stem: Find the \( x \)-intercept of the line \( [[A]]x+[[B]]y=[[C]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( B\) (the \( y\)-coefficient) instead of \( A\) in the denominator.
This drops a needed minus sign.
This inverts the fraction.
This multiplies by \( B\) instead of leaving the ratio alone.
This is off by one from the correct \( x\)-intercept.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( x\)-intercept.

stem: Find the \( y \)-intercept of the line \( [[A]]x+[[B]]y=[[C]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( A\) (the \( x\)-coefficient) instead of \( B\) in the denominator.
This drops a needed minus sign.
This inverts the fraction.
This multiplies by \( A\) instead of leaving the ratio alone.
This is off by one from the correct \( y\)-intercept.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( y\)-intercept.

stem: Line \( L \) is given by \( [[A]]x+[[B]]y=[[C]] \). Find the \( y \)-intercept of the line through \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) that is parallel to \( L \).
solution: \( L \) has slope \( m=-\frac{A}{B} \); a parallel line through \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) is \( y-[[y0]]=m(x-[[x0]]) \), which has \( y \)-intercept \( [[y0]]-m\cdot[[x0]]=[[y0]]+\frac{A}{B}\cdot[[x0]]=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign when solving for the intercept.
This uses the perpendicular slope instead of the parallel (same) slope.
This forgets to add the point's \( y \)-coordinate.
This uses the perpendicular slope with the correct sign convention, still the wrong relationship.
This is off by one from the correct \( y\)-intercept.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( y\)-intercept.

stem: Line \( L \) is given by \( [[A]]x+[[B]]y=[[C]] \). Find the \( y \)-intercept of the line through \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) that is perpendicular to \( L \).
solution: \( L \) has slope \( -\frac{A}{B} \), so a perpendicular line has slope \( m=\frac{B}{A} \). Through \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) this is \( y-[[y0]]=m(x-[[x0]]) \), with \( y \)-intercept \( [[y0]]-m\cdot[[x0]]=[[y0]]-\frac{B}{A}\cdot[[x0]]=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign when solving for the intercept.
This uses the parallel (same) slope instead of the perpendicular slope.
This forgets to negate the reciprocal slope.
This uses only the magnitude of the perpendicular slope without a sign.
This is off by one from the correct \( y\)-intercept.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( y\)-intercept.

stem: Find the \( x \)-coordinate of the intersection point of \( y=[[m1]]x+[[b1]] \) and \( y=[[m2]]x+[[b2]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the numerator.
This adds the slopes in the denominator instead of subtracting.
This adds the intercepts in the numerator instead of subtracting.
This reports only the difference of slopes, not a solved coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.

stem: Find the \( y \)-coordinate of the intersection point of \( [[A1]]x+[[B1]]y=[[C1]] \) and \( [[A2]]x+[[B2]]y=[[C2]] \).
solution: Solving the two equations simultaneously gives \( y=[[answer]] \) (by elimination or Cramer's rule).
distractor_rationales: This uses a Cramer's-rule numerator/denominator with the wrong sign.
This uses a naive (and generally wrong) ratio of the constants over the \( y \)-coefficients.
This has both the numerator and denominator sign-flipped from the correct value.
This reports only the difference of \( x\)-coefficients, not a solved coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct \( y\)-coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( y\)-coordinate.

stem: For which value of \( k \) are the lines \( [[A]]x+[[B]]y=[[C]] \) and \( [[D]]x+ky=[[E]] \) parallel?
solution: The slopes are \( -\frac{A}{B} \) and \( -\frac{D}{k} \); setting them equal gives \( Ak=DB \), so \( k=\dfrac{DB}{A}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( A\) and \( D\) together instead of solving the proportion correctly.
This negates the correct value.
This swaps the roles of \( D\) and \( A\) in the ratio.
This inverts the correct ratio entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k\).
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value of \( k\).

stem: For which value of \( k \) are the lines \( [[A]]x+[[B]]y=[[C]] \) and \( [[D]]x+ky=[[E]] \) perpendicular?
solution: The slopes are \( -\frac{A}{B}\) and \( -\frac{D}{k}\); perpendicularity requires their product to be \( -1 \), so \( \frac{AD}{Bk}=-1 \Rightarrow Bk=-AD \Rightarrow k=-\dfrac{AD}{B}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required minus sign.
This inverts the ratio before negating.
This swaps which coefficient is divided by which before negating.
This is off by one from a sign-flipped version of the answer.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k\).
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value of \( k\).

stem: For what value of \( k \) are the points \( ([[x1]],[[y1]]) \), \( ([[x2]],k) \), and \( ([[x3]],[[y3]]) \) collinear?
solution: Collinearity requires the slope from \( ([[x1]],[[y1]]) \) to \( ([[x2]],k) \) to equal the slope from \( ([[x2]],k) \) to \( ([[x3]],[[y3]]) \); solving \( (k-[[y1]])([[x3]]-[[x2]])=([[y3]]-k)([[x2]]-[[x1]]) \) gives \( k=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This averages the two given \( y \)-coordinates instead of solving the collinearity condition.
This adds the two given \( y \)-coordinates instead of solving for \( k\).
This reports the overall slope between the two known points rather than the required \( y \)-value.
This reports a raw difference instead of a coordinate value.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k\).
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value of \( k\).
